results.content:
EuniAI

### TUA-Bench: A Benchmark for General-Purpose Terminal-Use Agents [...] As large language models and harness frameworks continue to advance, agents operating in terminals are increasingly capable of performing a broader range of general computer-use tasks beyond coding. However, existing benchmarks do not adequately evaluate general-purpose terminal computer-use agents (TUAs): general computer-use benchmarks primarily target graphical user interfaces (GUIs), whereas terminal-based benchmarks largely emphasize technical and programming-centric workflows historically native to the shell. We introduce TUA-Bench, a general-purpose benchmark for terminal-use agents. TUA-Bench includes 120 real-world tasks across five task families, covering routine digital activities-including document editing, email management, and live-web information seeking-as well as [...] management, and live-web information seeking-as well as scientific and engineering workflows co-designed with PhD-level domain experts that require specialized software. This breadth distinguishes TUA-Bench from prior shell-focused or domain-specific benchmarks. Each task is manually designed, runs in a real terminal with a deterministic setup script, and is evaluated by an execution-based scoring protocol. We find that the strongest frontier agent, Claude Code with Claude Opus 4.8 max reasoning effort, achieves 65.8% overall performance, with substantial gaps across both tracks. By providing a broad and realistic evaluation of terminal-use capabilities, TUA-Bench aims to accelerate the transition from narrow, task-specific assistants to general-purpose agents capable of operating

# TUA-Bench: A Benchmark for General-Purpose Terminal-Use Agents

facebook

## Abstract

TUA-Bench presents a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating general-purpose terminal-use agents across diverse digital activities and specialized workflows, revealing significant performance gaps among current frontier agents.

results.title: TUA-Bench: New Benchmark for Terminal LLM Agents - YouTube
results.content: Welcome to the AI research roundup. I'm Alex. A paper from the hugging face trending list published just 4 days ago on June 26th, 2026 shows that even the top artificial intelligence agent achieves only a 66% success rate on everyday terminal tasks. The paper is titled TUA bench, a benchmark for general purpose terminal use agents. It introduces 120 manually curated tasks spanning from office work to complex scientific simulations. And for those interested in the implementation, the authors have shared their code on GitHub. Well, figure 1 maps out the complete evaluation workflow of TUA bench. The benchmark translates everyday tasks from a graphical user interface to a command line interface, forcing agents to operate purely through terminal commands. Once the agent finished running, the [...] In this AI Research Roundup episode, Alex discusses the paper: 'TUA-Bench: A Benchmark for General-Purpose Terminal-Use Agents' Current evaluations for computer-use agents focus heavily on visual GUI coordinates or narrow developer-centric programming workflows, leaving a gap for general-purpose terminal-based benchmarks. To bridge this gap, the authors introduce TUA-Bench, a new benchmark featuring 120 manually curated tasks designed specifically for terminal-use agents. These tasks span five broad categories, translating everyday digital tasks from GUI formats into command-line interface workflows with strict human verification. Additionally, the benchmark integrates professional scientific challenges co-designed with PhD-level experts utilizing specialized CLI software like OpenFOAM [...] Bench stands apart from existing evaluations by combining a terminal interface with a very broad task scope. While traditional terminal benchmarks focus purely on software engineering, Tiwa Bench includes office, web, and scientific tasks totaling 120 unique challenges. It is also the only command line benchmark here that supports multimodal tasks, meaning the agent can process visual inputs alongside standard terminal text. So, table 3 presents the main benchmarking results, highlighting how different models and agent scaffolds, which are the software rappers that manage how an AI interacts with the terminal, perform. Looking at the model sweep in the top section, GPT 5.5 achieves a 60% success rate. Moving to the bottom section, Claude Code paired with Claude Opus 4.8 reaches the

results.title: Snorkel AI Interview - A chat with the Terminal-Bench team - YouTube
results.content: and he was like I think you could release a benchmark with 20 tasks. I don't think people care how many tasks you and we were like no that's crazy like we need lots of tasks. And he was like the bigger question is just how are they going to use it? Will they be able to adopt it? So we went and talked to a bunch of different agent developers like open hands even manis who has this general purpose agent that uses a terminal terminal and the consistent feedback we got from all of them is we don't want more tasks we want to know how in the world we're supposed to evaluate our agent with its current tools and at that time we had not allowed people to install agents into the container to evaluate them and we ended up having to make that compromise where installing being able to install and run [...] this is that if your API calls are particularly slow or your agent runs particularly slow or your hardware is slow, then you're more likely to time out because the agent just can't operate as quickly in the environment. This is unfortunate, but the reality is that we do need some way of telling the container that it's done, that the agent is probably stuck in some loop, that it's not going to actually solve the problem, and we need to terminate and move on. You asked about the compromises that we had to make and the affordances that we designed into the benchmark. And the reason that this is a good example is that there are probably more elegant ways of doing this that just wouldn't be compatible with an arbitrary agent. So, like one thing you might want to do is limit the number of turns
